original_abstract:
This study provides a comparative analysis of copyright law across three major global jurisdictions: the European Union (EU), the United States (US), and the People’s Republic of China (China). By mapping the fundamental similarities and differences between these legal regimes, the research investigates the drivers behind the observed patterns of divergence and convergence. My findings suggest that international harmonization efforts, particularly the strictures of the Berne Convention, account for many shared legal features. In the case of China, economic motivations and external trade pressures have led to a copyright framework that is now remarkably similar to those of the US and EU, despite a vastly different historical and cultural heritage.

Nevertheless, significant differences persist, primarily rooted in the theoretical foundations of copyright doctrine. While the US maintains a largely utilitarian approach, the EU tends to emphasize "moral rights" stemming from the author’s personality. The traditional divide between common-law and civil-law systems also plays a role in how statutory law and judicial precedent are utilized. Interestingly, while China is structurally a civil-law system, its underlying rationale for protection and its practical reliance on precedent share surprising commonalities with the US common-law approach. Conversely, China aligns more closely with the EU regarding the statutory recognition of moral rights. Ultimately, this study illustrates how global legal transplants and domestic institutional needs continue to reshape the boundaries of intellectual property.
humanized_abstract:
Abstract The article presents the result of the comparative research carried out on copyright law across the three main global jurisdictions—EU, US, and China. In accordance with the research methodology defined within this field of study, it identifies, within these copyrights, both the elements of similarity and their contrary of the form of dissimilarity by evaluating the variables of this study, which explain the dynamics behind convergence and divergence. Results obtained show that both EU and US copyright legislations, as a consequence of an international harmonization required under the Berne Convention rules, are, at the level of regulation of literary and artistic works, to a great extent identical to each other, and that, in accordance with both economic and political considerations related to a series of foreign exchange processes within the framework of international commerce and having been constrained by significant changes in international agreements, particularly the adjustments in terms of copyright protection for foreign countries introduced in 2001 in China legislation, these countries own a very recent and highly elaborate and innovative copyright law, one which is increasingly aligning its principles on those in force in the USA and the EU, despite different ideological and cultural references.

Even though there are a number of similarities between these three systems, there are, however, many differences, the main reason for which is that the theory of copyright law differs significantly from one system to another. While the US Copyright law is heavily based on a utilitarian approach, which is shaped to a great extent by the personal rights of the author derived from the author’s personality—the so-called ‘moral rights’—in the EU system the personal rights of the author have far more weight and the common law/civil law distinction also plays a major role in the application of copyright law. There are a number of features in the Chinese copyright law and the way it is enforced in practice which resemble very much those of the US Copyright law. While China is formally a civil law system, in practice it applies in the field of copyright law certain principles which are similar to those of common law and rely on certain decisions of the courts which are good law even many years after they were decided. In certain respects, however, China follows the EU system, especially when it comes to the principles of copyright laid down in the copyright statute and the moral rights of the author. The present study offers a unique opportunity to see at work the interplay between the global process of the transplating different IP laws to different systems, and the national factors which affect the degree of success of these transplants.

document.output:

document.input:
This study provides a comparative analysis of copyright law across three major global jurisdictions: the European Union (EU), the United States (US), and the People’s Republic of China (China).
